preFix.title: Washing and care habits that extend garment life
preFix.subtitle: Cooler temperatures, gentler cycles, and drying choices that keep fabric longer
preFix.excerpt: The average UK garment is worn 7 times before disposal. The single biggest driver of premature fabric degradation is overwashing at high temperatures with excess detergent. Washing at 30°C instead of 60°C, selecting the gentle cycle, and drying flat rather than tumble-drying extends the functional life of most garments by 2 to 4 years. This covers the evidence and the practical changes that have most impact.
preFix.sourceNotes: WRAP Valuing Our Clothes report 2012 and 2020 update. Which? washing machine performance testing at 30°C vs 40°C vs 60°C. Textile Exchange fibre performance research.

content.text: Garments degrade in the wash three ways. Heat weakens fibres, shrinks fabric, and damages elastic. Friction causes fibre loss and
. Excess soap stiffens fibres and bleach degrades them. Most garments need far less heat and spin than they get.
The changes with the most impact

Wash at 30°C. Which? testing shows modern biological detergents at 30°C remove food and body soil stains as effectively as 40°C for standard loads. The lower temperature has a measurable effect on fabric life: fibres in a 30°C cycle absorb 20 to 30% less mechanical stress than at 60°C because the heat weakens fibre-to-fibre bonds before agitation.

Use the gentle or delicate cycle for knitwear, stretchy fabrics, and anything with a logo print or embellishment. The reduced spin speed (600 to 800 rpm versus 1200 to 1600 rpm on cotton) makes a larger difference than temperature for pilling and shape retention.

Use the correct detergent dose. The dose marked on the bottle is for a full load of heavily soiled items. For a half-load of lightly used clothes, use half to two-thirds of the recommended dose. Excess detergent does not improve cleaning. It leaves residue in fibres that causes stiffness and attracts more dirt.

Dry flat or on a hanger rather than tumble drying. Tumble dryer heat (typically 70 to 85°C drum temperature) is significantly more damaging than a 40°C wash cycle. Cotton shrinks; elastane (Lycra) degrades; printed designs crack. If tumble drying is necessary, use the low-heat setting and remove garments slightly damp to finish air-drying.
Turn garments inside out before washing to protect surface fibres, dyes, and prints from direct agitation and detergent contact.

Most outer garments (trousers, jumpers, jackets) do not need washing after every wear. Airing on a hanger for 24 hours after wearing removes most odour-causing bacteria and refreshes fabric. A T-shirt worn against the skin for a day is correctly washed after one wear; a jumper worn over a T-shirt is not. Reducing wash frequency from weekly to every 2 to 3 wears for outer layers extends garment life by the same factor as temperature reduction.

initialFindings.message: tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
paragraph reads at grade 11.7 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
initialFindings.snippet: Turn garments inside out before washing to protect surface fibres, dyes, and prints from direct agitation and detergent contact.
